headline: Human-readable certificate inspection joins the toolkit as pki.inspect.
summary: pki.inspect renders a certificate as a familiar OpenSSL-style text report -- the pure-JavaScript equivalent of openssl x509 -text -- so an operator can ingest a PEM or DER blob and read exactly what it contains without shelling out to OpenSSL. certificate(input) accepts a PEM string, a DER Buffer, or an already-parsed certificate and returns the version, serial number, signature algorithm, the issuer and subject distinguished names, the validity window, the public-key details (curve or modulus size plus the raw point/modulus), every decoded extension with its critical flag, and the signature. It renders purely from the toolkit's own strict parser and two-way OID registry -- no OpenSSL dependency and no second, drift-prone naming table -- so it names extension and algorithm OIDs that an OpenSSL build shows only as raw octets (a Fulcio certificate's build-provenance extensions, for instance, render as their values rather than a hex dump). The format is intentionally stable and OpenSSL-familiar rather than byte-identical to any one OpenSSL release (those disagree across versions -- 3.5 and 4.0 differ in hex wrap width and the EC key line), and rendering is best-effort: a malformed individual extension falls back to a hex dump rather than failing the whole report. Verified against openssl x509 -text (OpenSSL 4.0.1) as an independent oracle: the decoded values agree field for field.

Added: pki.inspect.certificate(input) renders a certificate (a PEM string, a DER Buffer, or a pki.schema.x509.parse result) as a human-readable OpenSSL-x509-text-style report, composed over the strict X.509 parser, the shared RFC 5280 extension decoders, and the two-way OID registry. Standard extensions (basic constraints, key usage, extended key usage with purpose names, subject/issuer alternative names, subject and authority key identifiers, and more) decode to their content; an extension with no decoder is named from the registry and shown as its string or a hex dump. A value that is not a certificate, DER, or PEM throws inspect/bad-input; malformed certificate bytes throw inspect/bad-certificate; a malformed single extension never sinks the report. The error taxonomy gains InspectError (inspect/*). RFC 5280.
